question_text: Which statement best explains why a parallel circuit allows current to continue flowing to some bulbs even if one bulb breaks, but a series circuit does not?

options.label: A
options.text: Parallel circuits have lower resistance, so electricity is harder to stop

options.label: B
options.text: In parallel circuits, current has alternative paths to flow through, so breaking one component doesn't stop current reaching others

options.label: C
options.text: Series circuits have higher voltage, which is why they break more easily

options.label: D
options.text: Parallel circuits store more energy, so current can bypass broken components

explanation: In a parallel circuit, each component is on its own branch with its own complete path from the power supply. If one branch breaks, current simply flows through the other branches. In a series circuit, there is only one path, so breaking one component stops all current flow.
